perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party’s obligations imposed pursuant to this Agreement will continue for two years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party’s obligations imposed pursuant to this Agreement will cease one year from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year creates a legal contradiction if trade secret protection laws in California (where Emergent Group, Inc. is located) offer a longer protection period, especially if the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year, the contract fails to adequately protect trade secrets that would legally be protected for a longer duration under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from two years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state this contradicts trade secret protection laws in California (CUTSA), which protect trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. The scraped law snippet 1, California Code, CIV 3426, confirms the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in California. By limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year, the contract fails to adequately protect trade secrets that would legally be protected for a longer duration under California law. This creates an Omission Legal Contradiction because the modified contract removes essential information, specifically the mandated trade secret protection, making it non-compliant with CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will limit the unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by using a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause "the same degree of care...as the Receiving Party uses to protect its own confidential information" reduces the standard of care required. If the information qualifies as a trade secret, this could violate trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy to qualify for trade secret protection. The original text implied a higher standard by referencing the Receiving Party's own confidential information protection practices. Removing this may render the protection inadequate under CUTSA, increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification omits a critical component of the standard of care for protecting confidential information. The original text required the Receiving Party to use at least the same degree of care they use to protect their own confidential information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care. The changed text only requires a reasonable degree of care. The Law Explanation states that the CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy for trade secret protection, and that the original text implied a higher standard. By removing the reference to the Receiving Party's own practices, the changed text potentially weakens trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', Snippet 1 confirms that CUTSA exists. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists because the modification reduces the standard of care and may render the protection inadequate under CUTSA, potentially leading to trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information, together with all copies thereof in the possession, custody or control of the Receiving Party, without retaining any copies.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all documents or other materials furnished by the Disclosing Party constituting Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "together with all copies thereof in the possession, custody or control of the Receiving Party, without retaining any copies" allows the Receiving Party to retain copies of confidential information. This may violate trade secret protection laws if the information meets the definition of a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Allowing retention of copies undermines this requirement, increasing the risk of misappropriation. The act of maintaining copies itself could be interpreted as misappropriation depending on the intent and use of those copies.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of "together with all copies thereof in the possession, custody or control of the Receiving Party, without retaining any copies" directly contradicts the requirement to maintain secrecy as required by CUTSA. The modified text allows the Receiving Party to keep copies of the Confidential Information, thereby undermining efforts to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation states: "The CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Allowing retention of copies undermines this requirement, increasing the risk of misappropriation."

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information, regardless of the form in which it appears, may include, without limitation, information about customers, business and marketing plans, financial data, expertise of employees, forecasts and strategies that is related to the business of the Disclosing Party and is of the type of information that would reasonably be deemed to be confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information may include information about customers, business and marketing plans, financial data, expertise of employees, forecasts and strategies that is related to the business of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "regardless of the form in which it appears" and "is of the type of information that would reasonably be deemed to be confidential" weakens the definition of Confidential Information. This makes it harder to claim trade secret protection for information not explicitly listed, violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By removing the phrase 'is of the type of information that would reasonably be deemed to be confidential', the contract creates ambiguity around what is protectable, potentially excluding information that would otherwise qualify for trade secret protection under CUTSA due to its inherent confidential nature.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits key phrases from the original definition of Confidential Information, specifically removing "regardless of the form in which it appears" and "is of the type of information that would reasonably be deemed to be confidential." This omission weakens the scope of what is considered confidential. The Law Explanation states that this change creates ambiguity around what is protectable, potentially excluding information that would otherwise qualify for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant law as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, indicating that the altered definition could undermine protections afforded under this act by making it more difficult to assert that certain information is indeed a trade secret. The omission aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, as a key aspect of consumer protection (trade secret protection) is weakened by removing legally relevant phrases.
